caveats: a: BLS publishes only a broad 513100 occupation mix, not exact 513199 or the narrowed repeat-program population.
a: The residual code may have a materially different mix of creators, editors, sales staff, digital workers, and outsourced production.
a: Occupation shares do not measure task-level exposure or current automation.
rho: No representative source measures AI realization in 513199.
rho: The residual code's format and product heterogeneity makes one implementation rate especially uncertain.
rho: Faster content production may expand catalogs and customization rather than reduce labor.
e: The frozen dataset declares no defensible LMM-band firm count.
e: The official residual category does not measure repeat-program revenue, normalized EBITDA, or transferability.
e: Copyright ownership can reside in a founder-dependent catalog that is transferable as an asset but not as an operating service firm.
s5: Gallup measures intentions among employer-business owners across industries, not completed publisher transfers.
s5: The proxy does not isolate LMM firms, rights-based businesses, or the repeat-program lens.
s5: Catalog and copyright asset sales may not be qualifying control transfers.
q: No representative source measures automation-benefit retention for 513199.
q: Physical wholesale, commissioned work, licensing, and direct digital sales have materially different pass-through mechanisms.
q: Productivity gains may fund more catalog breadth and customer-specific versions rather than retained profit.
d5: BLS projects employment for broad 513100, not constant-price demand for exact 513199.
d5: The residual category's niches may have opposite demand trajectories.
d5: Employment can fall through outsourcing or productivity while demand remains stable.
o: Official scope establishes publishing and copyright control, not the future operator-required share.
o: Operator need varies materially between rights-intensive licensed catalogs and simple commodity calendars or art prints.
o: A publisher may retain legal accountability while platforms and software perform most operating work.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Retailers, distributors, institutions, licensors, and business customers buy or license copyrighted art prints, calendars, and other published works through wholesale orders, repeat catalogs, seasonal programs, fixed projects, royalties, subscriptions where applicable, or digital licenses. Publishers acquire or create rights, curate editions, prepare content and metadata, market titles, manage customer programs, and coordinate physical or electronic distribution.
lens.deviation_from_default: The residual code is structurally heterogeneous and includes art publishers, calendar publishers, physical and electronic formats, and one-off product sales. For a coherent fixed-lens screen, it is narrowed to firms with repeat external-customer publishing or licensing programs; no content niche was selected for attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Newspaper, magazine, book, directory, mailing-list, music, software, and greeting-card publishers classified elsewhere; one-off direct-to-consumer art or calendar sellers without repeat customer programs; independent artists self-publishing their own work; printers without publishing rights; educational test developers not commonly known as publishers; shells, captive units, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the target normalized-EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market all-other publishers that provide recurring or repeat publishing, catalog, licensing, or content programs to external retailers, distributors, institutions, or business customers, including repeat art-print, calendar, reference-artifact, and other nonexcluded print or electronic publishing programs.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: May 2023 OEWS for broad NAICS 513100 reports management at 12.78%, business and financial operations at 10.68%, computer and mathematical at 8.71%, arts/design/media at 29.00%, sales at 13.37%, office support at 16.13%, and production at 3.84% of employment; Census defines 513199 as residual publishers operating in print or electronic form. Transformation: mapped portions of creative drafting and adaptation, editorial and metadata work, analysis, sales support, and administration to exposed tasks, with low exposure assigned to final curation, rights judgment, negotiation, relationships, and physical approval. Error direction: broad publishing may overstate exposure if 513199 contains more artisanal physical work, or understate it if the exact code is more digital and outsources production.
caveats: BLS publishes only a broad 513100 occupation mix, not exact 513199 or the narrowed repeat-program population.
The residual code may have a materially different mix of creators, editors, sales staff, digital workers, and outsourced production.
Occupation shares do not measure task-level exposure or current automation.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study of 513199 firms split among art, calendar, other physical, and electronic publishers, with current automation separated from new AI capability, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Image and copy adaptation, editorial cleanup, metadata, rights search support, catalog assembly, layout variants, market analysis, sales materials, customer support, and routine administration are exposed to AI. Final curation, originality and rights judgment, creator relationships, licensing negotiation, physical-quality approval, and accountable publication decisions remain human-led.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS projects broad NAICS 513100 employment from 268,500 in 2024 to 244,600 in 2034, an 8.9% decline; Census identifies 513199 as residual print or electronic publishing including art and calendar publishers. Transformation: converted the broad ten-year employment decline into a modestly declining five-year demand anchor and widened the range substantially for exact-code niche and format differences. Error direction: the employment proxy may overstate demand decline where productivity or outsourcing drives job losses, or understate it for physical categories displaced by digital and direct-creator channels.
caveats: BLS projects employment for broad 513100, not constant-price demand for exact 513199.
The residual category's niches may have opposite demand trajectories.
Employment can fall through outsourcing or productivity while demand remains stable.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price revenue and unit series for exact 513199 split by art prints, calendars, other products, physical versus electronic format, customer type, and repeat versus one-off programs would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Calendars, decorative art, licensed images, and specialty published artifacts retain recurring uses, while smartphones, direct creator platforms, digital substitutes, retailer consolidation, and print-channel pressure can reduce the current service basket. Broad publishing employment is projected to decline, but exact-code demand could diverge sharply by niche and format.

caveats: The frozen dataset declares no defensible LMM-band firm count.
The official residual category does not measure repeat-program revenue, normalized EBITDA, or transferability.
Copyright ownership can reside in a founder-dependent catalog that is transferable as an asset but not as an operating service firm.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level file for 513199 linking format and product type to normalized EBITDA, repeat B2B revenue, customer concentration, rights ownership, creator dependence, fulfillment model, and transferable contracts would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: A subset of specialty publishers has repeat retailer or institutional programs, controlled rights, transferable catalogs, and independent operations. Eligibility is low because the residual NAICS category is product-based, many firms depend on individual creators or one-off works, customer programs may not be recurring outsourced services, and the frozen LMM firm-count input is unavailable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 51 ($506,769,522,547) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 51 ($2,139,449,301,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 51. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3593 (margins.json[513]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Software (System & Application) EBITDA/Sales 35.93% (NAICS 2022 513 = publishing incl. software)); revenue/employee $588,729 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 51); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Official scope establishes publishing and copyright control, not the future operator-required share.
Operator need varies materially between rights-intensive licensed catalogs and simple commodity calendars or art prints.
A publisher may retain legal accountability while platforms and software perform most operating work.
o.change_evidence: Customer-cohort studies comparing specialty publishers with direct creators, print-on-demand platforms, retailer self-publishing, and software-first licensing services on cost, quality, rights incidents, delivery, sales, and renewal would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Customers still need an accountable publisher to control or license rights, curate editions, manage provenance, prepare production-ready assets, coordinate distribution, and stand behind quality and delivery. Creator-direct platforms, marketplace infrastructure, generative content, print-on-demand, retailer self-service, and software can eliminate much of the traditional operator role for simpler works.

caveats: No representative source measures automation-benefit retention for 513199.
Physical wholesale, commissioned work, licensing, and direct digital sales have materially different pass-through mechanisms.
Productivity gains may fund more catalog breadth and customer-specific versions rather than retained profit.
q.change_evidence: Matched contract and account evidence across art, calendar, and other specialty publishers covering prices, royalties, platform and distribution fees, renewal terms, returns, service levels, gross margin, and workflow savings would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Publishers may retain workflow savings within fixed wholesale prices, license fees, seasonal programs, or digital products until renegotiation, and can use productivity to expand titles without proportional labor. Retention is reduced by retailer and distributor bargaining, creator royalties, competitive bidding, low switching costs for generic content, platform fees, and reinvestment in customization and promotion.

caveats: No representative source measures AI realization in 513199.
The residual code's format and product heterogeneity makes one implementation rate especially uncertain.
Faster content production may expand catalogs and customization rather than reduce labor.
rho.change_evidence: Workflow-level deployment studies from art, calendar, and specialty publishers measuring labor hours, revision cycles, rights incidents, customer acceptance, sell-through, implementation costs, and service quality would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI can be integrated into concept development, image and text variants, metadata, rights-search triage, catalog production, localization, marketing, forecasting, and account support within five years. Implementation is constrained by inconsistent legacy assets, provenance and copyright risk, creator agreements, customer approvals, low-volume specialty workflows, integration cost, and the need for human editorial accountability.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's fall-2024 survey reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; Census describes publishers as firms that typically own or license copyrighted works. Transformation: used 22% as a remote upper intention anchor and discounted for noncompletion, founder dependence, rights defects, ineligible one-off publishers, and asset-only deals. Error direction: broad intentions likely overstate qualifying completed transfers, while strategic acquisitions of durable catalogs or licenses could make the low case conservative.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions among employer-business owners across industries, not completed publisher transfers.
The proxy does not isolate LMM firms, rights-based businesses, or the repeat-program lens.
Catalog and copyright asset sales may not be qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched cohort of eligible 513199 firms and completed operating-company control transfers from private-deal databases, broker records, lender files, and catalog-acquisition announcements would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Eligible publishers may transfer when owners retire or buyers acquire catalogs, licenses, and customer programs. Completion is limited by creator dependence, uncertain rights chains, small scale, customer concentration, and the possibility that only assets rather than control of an operating firm are sold.

principal_driver: The principal driver is AI exposure across creative adaptation, editorial, rights-support, catalog, sales, and administrative workflows in repeat publishing programs.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is an unknown firm pool inside a residual category where many businesses do not fit a recurring outsourced-service or transferable operating-company model.

sources.supports: Official 513199 scope, art and calendar examples, print and electronic formats, exclusions, and publisher copyright, licensing, and distribution role; lens, a, e, s5, q, d5, and o

title: All Other Publishers
